coredata.dc:title: Computationally exploring the role of S-dopant and S-linker in activating the catalytic efficiency of graphene quantum dot for ORR
coredata.prism:issueName: Advanced Catalyst for Clean Environment and Green Energy
coredata.dc:creator: Banerjee, Paramita
Das, G.P.
Thapa, Ranjit
coredata.dc:description: To enhance the catalytic efficiency of graphene quantum dot (GQD) for ORR, the role of S-dopant and S-linker is explored here by utilizing the first-principles density functional theory-based approach. The ORR efficiency is found to be changed on different active sites with the variation of dopant position and dopant configuration on the sulfurized GQD. Charge transfer from the dopant site to the active sites and to the intermediates, as well as the binding energy of intermediates on the active sites play an important role to realize the potential determining step, onset potential and the overpotential for ORR. The increase in the doping concentration of S or the presence of oxygen is found to drastically enhance the ORR efficiency of GQD. Furthermore, S-linkers are exploited to connect two GQDs entangled or twisted with each other and resulting in a rather complex system. The combined effect of entangling, S-linkers and presence of a single N dopant at the meta position of the active site resulted in the adsorption of the intermediates with optimum binding strength. Consequently, the ORR activity is found to be enhanced, thereby resulting in a metal-free and cost-effective electrocatalyst with efficiency similar to the Pt-based metal catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalyst
Graphene quantum dot
ORR
Adsorption
DFT

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: Advanced Catalyst for Clean Environment and Green Energy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Geometrical structure and dopant configurations for single GQD
Bader charge transfer and electronic structure analysis
ORR activity on the SC_GQD, SE_GQD, SO2_GQD and S−OH_GQD systems
Role of S-linkers for ORR on entangled GQDs
Effect of N-doping on the ORR activity of 2S_GQD_Ent system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: PB and GPD thankfully acknowledges IACS for the financial and computational support. R.T. thanks Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences (BRNS) , India, (Grant Nos. 37(2)/20/14/2018-BRNS/37144 ) and Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), India , (Grant Nos. EMR/2016/004689 ) for the financial support. Authors thank High Performance Computing Centre, SRM Institute of Science and Technology for providing the computational facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Model GQD structure with all the dopant configurations considered in this manuscript. A-E represents the dopant types or dopant configuration. A = When S is doped at the centre, B = When S is doped at the edge, C = When double dopant (2S) is considered, D = When S is doped in presence of O2 i.e., SO2 doped, E = When S is doped in presence of OH i.e., S + OH doped. C0, C1 and C2 represents the substitutionally doped S atom, its nearest neighbour C atom and the second nearest neighbour C atom respectively, which are considered as the active sites for ORR for each dopant type.

Free energy profile along the intermediate steps of ORR in alkaline medium on the 2SE_GQD system considering (a) C0, (b) C 1, and (c) C2 as the active sites. The inset figures represent the optimized structures after OOH adsorption on C0, C1 and C2 respectively. (d) represents the atomic geometries and reaction formulas of each intermediate step of ORR on C1 atom. The colours of the balls are similar as mentioned in Fig. 1.
Spin polarized partial density of states (pDOS) of C0, C1 and C2 (a) before OOH adsorption and (b) after OOH adsorption on C0, C1 and C2 on the 2SE_GQD system. Here Fermi level is set to zero.

Free energy profile along the intermediate steps of ORR in alkaline medium on (a) SO2_GQD and (b) S−OH_GQD systems considering C1 as the active site in both the cases. The inset figures represent the optimized structures after OOH adsorption on C1 atom of the corresponding systems. The colours of the balls are similar as mentioned in Fig. 1.

(a) Top view and (b) Side view of the geometry optimized structures of two GQDs entangled with each other and connected via two S atoms i.e., S-linkers (2S_GQD_Ent system). (c) Free energy profile of the 2S_GQD_Ent system along the intermediate steps of ORR in alkaline medium by considering C 2 as the active site. The colours of the balls are similar as mentioned in Fig. 1.

(a) Top view of the geometry optimized structure of N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent system (when N atom is substitutionally doped at the meta position of C2 of the 2S_GQD_Ent system) after OOH adsorption. (b) Free energy profile of the N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent system along the intermediate steps of ORR in alkaline medium by considering C2 as the active site. The blue colour ball represents N atom. Other colours are similar as mentioned in Fig. 1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Sum DOS of C0, C1, C2, C3 and C4/CN before OOH adsorption and after OOH adsorption on C2 for (a) 2S-GQD_Ent and (b) N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent systems. Here Fermi level is set to zero.
Binding energy of the intermediates of ORR on the three active sites (C0, C1 and C2) of the 2SE_GQD systems.

row.entry: Binding energy value (eV) of intermediates during ORR

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bader charge state of the C0, C1 and C2 atoms on pristine GQD system as well as on the 2SE_GQD system before and after the OOH adsorption.
Binding energy of the intermediates of ORR on the C2 atom of the 2S_GQD_Ent and the N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent systems.

row.entry: Binding energy value (eV) of intermediates for ORR on C2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bader charge states of the C0, C1, C2, C3 and C4/CN atoms of the 2S_GQD_Ent and the N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent systems before and after the OOH adsorption on C2.

head.ce:title: Computationally exploring the role of S-dopant and S-linker in activating the catalytic efficiency of graphene quantum dot for ORR

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics, SRM University – AP, Amaravati, 522240, Andhra Pradesh, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To enhance the catalytic efficiency of graphene quantum dot (GQD) for ORR, the role of S-dopant and S-linker is explored here by utilizing the first-principles density functional theory-based approach. The ORR efficiency is found to be changed on different active sites with the variation of dopant position and dopant configuration on the sulfurized GQD. Charge transfer from the dopant site to the active sites and to the intermediates, as well as the binding energy of intermediates on the active sites play an important role to realize the potential determining step, onset potential and the overpotential for ORR. The increase in the doping concentration of S or the presence of oxygen is found to drastically enhance the ORR efficiency of GQD. Furthermore, S-linkers are exploited to connect two GQDs entangled or twisted with each other and resulting in a rather complex system. The combined effect of entangling, S-linkers and presence of a single N dopant at the meta position of the active site resulted in the adsorption of the intermediates with optimum binding strength. Consequently, the ORR activity is found to be enhanced, thereby resulting in a metal-free and cost-effective electrocatalyst with efficiency similar to the Pt-based metal catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: In the recent years, heteroatom doped carbon nanostructures have been emerged as the potential metal-free catalysts [1,2] for Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) [3–5] in the low temperature fuel cell. To circumvent the limitations of Pt-based metal catalysts, the advantages of metal-free catalysts in converting the chemical energy stored and carried by hydrogen [6–9] into electrical energy via ORR have been discussed extensively in the available literature. In this regard, carbon nanostructures have drawn significant attention due to their cost-effectivity, sufficient availability, medium binding energy (between physisorption and chemisorption) of the intermediates, limited self-oxidation and negligible surface poisoning by the reactive elements [10–12]. However, in the pristine form most of the carbon nanostructures show poor activity as the cathode material to capture the dioxygen, thereby indicating their inefficiency as electrocatalysts for ORR. Doping with heteroatoms [13–16] has been realized as an effective tool to increase the catalytic activity of different carbon-based nanomaterials by tuning their electronic structure.

Among the different heteroatom dopants on the carbon nanostructures, nitrogen is one of the most extensively studied elements for exploring the ORR activity. Earlier, it has already been reported both experimentally [17–19] and by first-principles density functional theory (DFT) based computational methodology [20–23] that the neighbouring carbon atoms of the dopant N (having high electronegativity: 3.04) induced with high charge densities and high spin densities serve as the active sites for efficient ORR. This is attributed to the more electrophilic nature of dioxygen (as compared to N) that can readily be adsorbed on those neighbouring carbon atoms by gaining the electronic charge density, thereby facilitating ORR with an efficiency even better than the Pt-based metal catalysts [24–27].

Apart from nitrogen, an upsurging interest has been found very recently to explore the ORR activity of the carbon nanostructures by using dopant with almost similar electronegativity with carbon such as sulfur (electronegativity: 2.04) [28,29] and selenium (electronegativity: 2.55) [30,31]. In 2015, Sun et al. [32] synthesized sulfur-doped carbon spheres via a solvothermal method. They have experimentally reported an improved ORR activity by investigating lower HO2 − production as well as a higher electron transfer number on these S-doped carbon systems as compared to those of the pristine carbon spheres. In another report, Jeon et al. [33] showed that the edge-sulfurized graphene nanoplatelets can be successfully generated by the ball-milling process from pristine graphite in the presence of sulfur. Both via experiment and via DFT based theoretical calculations they reported that the edge doping by S atoms strongly promotes the ORR activity of the graphene nanoplatelets through the most efficient four-electron pathway. In a recent report, Maiti et al., [34] showed both experimentally and computationally (DFT) that the edge doped sulfur on graphene nanoribbon turns out to be to a highly efficient electrocatalyst for ORR in presence of oxygen with a significantly low overpotential value (∼0.5 eV) that is comparable to the conventionally used Pt-based metal catalysts. In 2017, Lu et al. [35] studied the kinetic and thermodynamic behaviours of ORR on sulfur doped monovacancy graphene using DFT based approach and suggested by their calculations that the sulfur doped monovacancy graphene would be an efficient electrocatalyst for ORR.

In all the above-mentioned studies the origin of this enhanced ORR activity of carbon nanostructures via S doping is explained by the induction of a high spin density to the neighbouring carbon atoms of the dopant site. Furthermore, the co-doping or dual-doping of mesoporous graphene by sulfur and nitrogen [36,37] has been found to exhibit better catalytic efficiency for ORR as compared to that of the solely S-doped or solely N-doped graphene. In 2012, for the first time Liang et al. [36] reported the design and synthesis of N and S dual-doped graphene (N–S-G) in one step without the formation of any side products. They found a significantly higher value of reaction current for ORR with the N–S-G system as compared to that of the solely S-doped or solely N-doped graphene, thereby indicating more efficient ORR process with this dual heteroatom doped N–S-G system. Recently, Zhao et al. [37] evaluated the electrocatalytic activity of N and S dual-doped graphene by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) process which showed an electron transfer number of 3.75 ± 0.15, thereby ensuring the favourable four-electron pathway for ORR. Also, the maximum power density was obtained as 1368 m W
m
- 2
with the considered N and S dual-doped graphene. This power density value is significantly higher as compared to that with the Pt-based metal catalysts (1011 m W
m
- 2
) . This is attributed to the synergistic effect of inducing asymmetrical spin and charge densities by this dual doping of S and N. In another experimental report, Qu et al. [38] explained that the S-N-dual doping on the mesoporous carbon nanosheets can achieve unique electronic structures and exhibits tailored electrocatalytic activity for ORR. This might be attributed to the high concentrations of multiple dopants, abundant porous architecture and good electron transfer ability. Moreover, this S-N-dual doping is found to result in an enhanced tolerance to fuel crossover [36], thereby showing a high longevity in alkaline medium as compared to that of the Pt-based metal catalysts. All these findings motivated the researchers to further study the ORR activity of N–S dual doping on the graphene-based systems.

In addition to the induced charge density and spin density there are some other parameters such as the pz orbital occupancy and/or the configuration of atomic oxygen (i.e. whether enolate or epoxy), which play a significant role to enhance the reactivity of the active sites towards the intermediates [39]. As a consequence, the activation barrier is reduced and the kinetics of ORR on the heteroatom doped sp 2 hybridized graphene based planer structures is improved significantly. Despite an improved ORR activity, the origin of variation of the catalytic efficiency on different active sites of S-doped graphene with the change in the doping position and dopant configuration is not yet explored to its full potential. Moreover, a systematic investigation is still elusive on the catalytic activity of relatively complex systems where more than one carbon nanostructures are connected to each other generating a 2D surface derived 3D-nanostructure.

In the present work, primarily we have focused on exploring the catalytic efficiency of sulfurized graphene quantum dot (GQD) for the ORR via first-principles DFT based calculations. The prime objective of the present work is to explore a suitable metal-free electrocatalyst by investigating the role of S-dopant as well as S-linker to activate the catalytic efficiency of GQD. We have considered the effect of S-dopant on the catalytic activity by tuning the position (either edge doped or centre doped) and dopant configuration (either in presence of oxygen or double dopant). Furthermore, we have reported here how the S atoms act as the linkers between two GQDs which are entangled or twisted with each other, thereby resulting in a new and rather complex system that acts as a highly efficient metal-free and cost-effective electrocatalyst in presence of a single N dopant.

For all the geometry optimizations, total energy evaluations and electronic structure analysis we have carried out spin polarized DFT [40,41] based calculations with Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) [42,43] associated with the plane wave basis set. Projector-augmented wave (PAW) potentials [44] are employed to describe all the constituent elements (e.g., C, O, H, S and N). Generalized gradient approximation (GGA) is exploited for the exchange-correlation functional of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) [45]. All our calculations are carried out by considering a monolayer of GQD. In order to circumvent any undesirable interaction between the periodically repeating structures, the GQD is placed within an empty unit cell (vacuum) of dimension 30 × 30 × 30 Å. All the geometry optimizations and electronic structure analysis are performed by Γ (gamma) point calculations. A plane-wave energy cut-off of 500 eV is employed here. Charge transfer in all the atoms have been evaluated via Bader [46] charge analysis method. For a better treatment of the weakly interacting intermediate molecules in ORR, we have estimated van der Waals corrected total energies using Grimme’s DFT-D2 dispersion correction [47] as implemented in VASP. For the total energy calculations of the pristine as well as all the doped structures reported here, self-consistency has been achieved within 1 × 10−5 eV convergences in total energy. Ground state geometries have been optimized until the atomic forces (Hellman − Feynman forces) are minimized to 0.001 eV/Å. For visualisation of all the initial as well as the optimized geometries, the graphical software viz. Vesta [48] is used here.

For the ORR process, a detailed explanation of the mechanism to analyse the free energy profile along the intermediate reaction steps in alkaline medium by considering the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) and a short description of all the required parameters are given in the supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometrical structure and dopant configurations for single GQD
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the effect of S-dopant, we consider a monolayer of the planer GQD with almost square like shape consisting of total 68 carbon atoms. The edge carbon atoms are passivated with hydrogen in both armchair and zigzag direction. The full geometry optimized structure of this GQD is shown in Fig. S1 in the supplementary material. The dimension of this GQD along armchair direction is ∼14 Å and along zigzag direction is ∼13 Å. In the present report we have considered five different types or configurations of the dopant S, such as A: when S is doped at the centre, B: when S is doped at the edge, C: when double dopant (2S) at the edge is considered, D: when S is doped at the edge in presence of O2 i.e., SO2 doped, E: when S is doped at the edge in presence of OH i.e., S + OH doped. In all the above mentioned cases, S is substituionally doped i.e. S doping is done by replacing a carbon atom and the adjacent hydrogen atom. All the dopant configurations are considered individually along the zigzag direction on the same GQD shown in Fig. S1 of the supplementary material, Here the GQD system with dopant types A, B, C, D and E are designated as SC_GQD, SE_GQD, 2SE_GQD, SO2_GQD and S−OH_GQD respectively. In case of the SC_GQD, SE_GQD, SO2_GQD and S−OH_GQD systems the doping concentration of S is 1.47 %. Whereas, for 2SE_GQD system the doping concentration of S becomes 2.94 %. Fig. 1 represents the model GQD structure with all the dopant types or configurations (A–E) considered in the present study. Here C0, C1 and C2 represents the substitutionally doped S atom, its first nearest neighbour C atom and the second nearest neighbour C atom respectively which are considered as the active sites for ORR in the present study.

First, we investigate the ORR activity on the 2SE_GQD system i.e. when double dopant (2S) at the edge is considered. We have studied the free energy profile along the ORR pathway considering C0, C1 as well as C2 as the active sites, as shown in Fig. 2 (a), (b) and (c) respectively. The inset figures in the Fig. 2 (a), (b) and (c) represent the optimized structures after OOH adsorption on C0, C1 and C2 respectively. The atomic geometries and reaction formulas of each intermediate step of ORR while considering C1 atom as the active site is represented in Fig. 2 (d). Our aim is to explore how the ORR activity changes on different carbon atoms, in particular on the first (C1) and second (C2) nearest neighbour of the dopant site (C0) in the 2SE_GQD system. From Fig. 2 (d) it is clear that in the first reaction step, two H2O molecules (in liquid state), one O2 molecule and the 2SE_GQD surface are positioned adequately far apart from each other to restrict any interaction between them. The second step is essentially the first coupled proton and electron transfer (CPET) step where the O2 molecule intereacts with H generated from the H2O, thereby leading to the formation of the first intermediate molecule i.e. OOH. After formation this OOH gets adsorbed on the active site (C0/C1/C2) with “end-on” configuration (as shown in Fig. 2 (a), (b) and (c)) by forming either a SO bond (for C0) or a CO bond (for C1 and C2). After full geometry optimization we found the SO bond length as 1.88 Å considering C0 as the active site. On the other hand, while considering C1 or C2 as the active site for OOH adsorption in ORR, the corresponding CO bond length is found to be 1.46 Å or 1.51 Å respectively. From Fig. 2 (a), (b), and (c) it can be explained that at zero cell potential, this OOH adsorption step is endothermic (uphill in energy) for all the three cases (i.e., adsorption on C0/C1 or C2). This uphill in energy is the lowest (only 0.12 eV) for ORR on C1, which is followed by that on the C0 (0.45 eV). However, for ORR on C2 this endothermic nature of the free energy profile is significantly higher with an uphill in energy by 0.89 eV in the first CPET step. The next step is the 2nd CPET step in which the second electron is transferred. In this step, another H atom (again generated from the H2O) is found to interact with the previously adsorbed OOH on the considered active site (C0/C1/C2) of the 2SE_GQD surface. Interestingly, after full geometry optimization the OO bond is completely broken, thereby leading to the formation of a H2O molecule. Whereas, a single oxygen atom still remains attached to the active site. For all the three active sites considered here, i.e., for ORR on C0, C1 and C2 this reaction step is found to be strongly exothermic by 2.30 eV, 1.78 eV and 2.73 eV respectively at zero cell potential. This highly exothermic nature indicates the spontaneity of this ORR step and ensures that the second electron transfer does not lead to the formation of H2O2. In the next CPET step, the third electron is transferred, which results in the formation of the last intermediate of ORR i.e., OH which is adsorbed on the considered active site. This step again shows an endothermic nature for both C0 and C2 with an uphill in energy by 0.36 eV and 0.81 eV respectively. However, in case of ORR on C1 this 3rd CPET step shows an exothermic nature by 0.16 eV. In the last CPET step the 4th electron is transferred, thereby resulting in the formation of total 4OH− ions along with the isolated 2SE_GQD surface. This step shows an exothermic nature for C0 and C2 by 0.11 eV and 0.57 eV respectively. However, for ORR on C1, this last CPET step i.e. the desorption of final OH− ion shows a slightly endothermic nature with a small uphill in energy by only 0.10 eV. Furthermore, we have investigated the free energy profile for ORR on C0, C1 and C2 with an applied cell potential 0.40 V (equilibrium potential with respect to the SHE) and -0.83 V, as shown in Fig. 2 (a), (b) and (c) respectively.

Now, the maximal value of the applied cell potential at which all the intermediate reaction steps in the fee energy profile of ORR becomes exothermic (downhill in energy) and the ORR is expected to undergo spontaneously is known as the onset potential. In the present study for ORR on C0, C1 and C2 the onset potential values are found to be -0.45 eV, -0.12 eV and -0.89 eV respectively considering the SHE in alkaline medium. These values are evaluated from the highest uphill energy values in the free energy profile at zero cell potential. So, the corresponding overpotential values (SHE) are estimated as 0.85 eV, 0.52 eV and 1.29 eV for ORR on C0, C1 and C2 respectively. This estimated overpotential value on the C1 is in agreement with the earlier reported (both experimentally and computationally) results by Maiti et al. [34]. In all these three cases the first CPET step i.e., the formation and adsorption of OOH on the active site of the 2SE_GQD system is found to be the potential determining step. An electrocatalyst will show a significantly high efficiency for ORR if the overpotential value becomes comparable to that of the Pt-based metal catalysts i.e., < 0.6 eV. However, the metal-free electrocatalyst can be considered to be suitable for ORR even if it shows a moderate efficiency with overpotential value < 0.9 eV which is the acceptable range [39]. If the overpotential value becomes even higher than 0.9 eV, the electrocatalyst shows very limited activity and is not at all suitable for ORR. In the ideal ORR process, the maximum output voltage we can obtain is 1.23 eV. However, the estimated value of open-circuit voltage, in practice, is essentially lower than this. The difference i.e., the amount of cell potential which is lost during ORR is known as overpotential and the amount of cell potential which we actually gain as the output voltage is known as the onset potential. So, onset potential = 1.23 – overpotential

Hence, the higher the overpotential value, the lower the onset potential and so the ORR activity of the considered electrocatalyst. When the overpotential value becomes 0.9 eV, the estimated onset potential is only (1.23−0.9) eV =0.33 eV. This onset potential value is significantly low. So, when the overpotential value becomes higher than 0.9 eV, the onset potential becomes even lower than 0.33 eV and the ORR with the considered electrocatalyst becomes expensive and time consuming, thereby lowering the activity significantly. Hence, maximum limit of allowed overpotential value to estimate the ORR efficiency of a particular electrocatalyst has been fixed at 0.9 eV in the earlier reported results [39].

Now based on the estimated overpotential values it can be inferred that C1 is the best possible active site for ORR on 2SE_GQD system as the overpotential value is comparable to the conventionally used Pt-based metal catalysts. Whereas, C0 shows comperatively lower activity for ORR due to its higher overpotential value. However, ORR is not at all possible by considering C2 as the active site. This is attributed to the enhanced overpotential value which is even higher than 1.23 eV and hence, we will not obtain any energy in the output. Furthermore, to explore the possibility of CO poisoning on the 2SE_GQD surface, the binding strength of CO on the active sites is calculated. We found that CO can bind to the surface through week van der waals force only. This indicates very little chances of surface poisoning by CO.

In the free energy profile of ORR pathway the potential determining step to estimate the onset potential as well as the overpotential, essentially depends on the binding energy of the intermediates adsorbed on the surface. For an efficient ORR on a particular active site it is absolutely essential to adsorb the intermediate molecules with moderate binding energies. Since, in this system the OOH adsorption step is the potential determining step for ORR on C0, C1 and C2, it is highly crucial to explore the interaction characteristics of OOH with the considered active sites in order to understand the origin of the differences in catalytic activity between C0, C1 and C2. Accordingly, we have estimated the binding energy Δ
E B
of all the three intermediates (OOH, O and OH) with the active sites by using the following equation.
Δ
E B
A
= (
E
A ( S u r f . )
-
E
S u r f .
-
E
A
)
Here, ‘A’ represents the intermediate molecules i.e., OOH/O/OH,
E
A ( S u r f . )
represents the total energy of the intermediate molecule A after being adsorbed on the surface.
E
S u r f .
Represents the total energy of the free surface and
E
A
represents the total energy of an isolated intermediate molecule A, evaluated by insering within a vacuum of dimension 30 × 30 × 30 Å. The estimated binding energy values of all the intermediates on the three considered active sites are shown in Table 1 . We can see that the binding energy of OOH is the highest (-1.54) on C1 which is followed by C0 (-1.26 eV) and C2 (-0.78 eV). In case of C1 this value is exactly in the middle of the desired energy window (∼ -1.14 to −2 eV) required for the optimum catalytic activity. However, in case of C0 the value is closer to the lower limit of the desired range and in case of ORR on C2 the binding energy of OOH is significantly lower as compared to the above-mentioned range. Hence, a substantial amount of energy has to be employed to stabilize the OOH on C2 at ambient conditions. Consequently, the activation barrier as well as the overpotential value turns out to be significantly high, thereby preventing the spontaneity of ORR on C2. Our results imply that the binding energy of OOH is following exactly the opposite trend as that of the overpotential value on a particular active site and thereby playing an important role to get an idea about the overpotential value. The lower the binding energy of OOH on a particular active site (C0/C1/C2), the higher the overpotential value for ORR on the corresponding site.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bader charge transfer and electronic structure analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the nature of OOH binding on the active sites of the 2SE_GQD system, we have performed bader [46] charge analysis as well as the electronic structure calculations. Table 2 represents the bader charge state of the C0, C1 and C2 atoms on pristine GQD system and on the 2SE_GQD system before and after the OOH adsorption. From Table 2 we can explain that in spite of having almost similar electronegativity with carbon, the doped sulfur atom (C0) donates some electronic charges to the nearest carbon atom i.e., to the C1 atom. In case of the 2SE_GQD system both the C0 atoms donate electronic charges to the C1 atom and become cationic with charge states +0.22 and +0.20. Whereas, the charge state of C1 changes from +0.06 to -0.41 after gaining the electronic charges from the two doped S atoms (C0) on the 2SE_GQD system. As a consequence, the OOH molecule can easily be adsorbed on the C1 atom by grabbing some charge density. The charge state of the C1 atom further changes to +0.36 after adsorbing the OOH with a binding energy equal to -1.54 eV. However, comparatively smaller amount of charge is transferred to OOH after being adsorbed on the C0 and C2 atoms as shown in Table 2. As a consequence, the binding energy values of OOH on C0 and C2 become significantly lower as compared to that on the C1 atom, thereby resulting in a higher value of overpotential for ORR.

This charge transfer mechanism is further verified by the partial density of states (pDOS) analysis as shown in Fig. 3 . (a) and (b). From these figures it is evident that the reduction in electronic density of states near the Fermi level after the OOH adsorption is maximum for C1, which is followed by that of the C0 and C2. This reduction in electronic density of states is due to the charge transfer from the active sites (C0/C1/C2) to OOH. Earlier it is reported [32–34] that in the S doped graphene systems primarily the induction of high spin density on the neighbouring carbon atoms by the S dopant plays the most significant role to activate the catalytic efficiency for ORR. However, our results indicate that the charge transfer from the doped S atoms (C0) to the neighbouring carbon atoms also play an important role to enhance the catalytic activity of the 2SE_GQD system as compared to that of the pristine GQD. Furthermore, the difference in the overpotential value and catalytic activity between C0, C1 and C2 can also be explained by estimating the Bader charge states of the corresponding active sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR activity on the SC_GQD, SE_GQD, SO2_GQD and S−OH_GQD systems
ce:section.ce:para: We have also explored the ORR activity of the SC_GQD (single S atom doped at the centre of the GQD) and the SE_GQD (single S atom doped at the edge of the GQD) systems by following the same principle as mentioned in the previous section. We found that due to the higher amount of charge transfer the SC_GQD system shows an over activity for ORR to adsorb OOH and O on both the C0 and C1 atoms. Whereas, a negligible amount of charge transfer leads to a very low activity for ORR on C0, C1 and C2 of the SE_GQD system. The ORR activity on the SC_GQD and on the SE_GQD systems are explained in details within the supplementary material. Overall, our results on the SC_GQD and SE_GQD clearly indicate that these systems are not suitable as ORR catalysts due to the high over-potential values. However, it is indeed interesting to find that when the doping concentration of S is increased that is double S dopant is considered at the edge, the resultant 2SE_GQD system acts as an excellent electrocatalyst for ORR with a significantly lower overpotential value. This is attributed to the optimum charge transfer from the dopant site (C0) to the active sites, which leads to the moderate binding energy values of the intermediates on the active sites of the 2SE_GQD as compared to those of the SC_GQD and SE_GQD systems.

Furthermore, we have explored the effect of S dopant in presence of oxygen as well as in presence of OH on the ORR efficiency of GQD. Accordingly, we have studied the SO2_GQD and S−OH_GQD systems. Fig. 4 represents the free energy profiles along the intermediate steps of ORR in alkaline medium on (a) SO2_GQD and (b) S−OH_GQD systems considering C1 as the active site in both the cases. The inset figures represent the optimized structures after OOH adsorption on C1 atom of the corresponding systems. The bader charge state of C0, C1 and C2 before and after the OOH adsorption on C1 and the binding energy of the intermediates on C1 for both the systems are shown in Table S3 and S4 respectively of the supplementary material. In order to have a more clear idea about the charge transfer to adsorb OOH, we have carried out the electronic structure analysis by plotting the sum of partial density of states (pDOS) of the C0, C1 and C2 before OOH adsorption and after OOH adsorption on C1 for both the SO2_GQD and S−OH_GQD systems, as shown in Fig. S3 (a) and (b) respectively of the supplementary material. The SO2_GQD system exhibits a significantly high efficiency for ORR with an overpotential value as low as 0.54 eV. Our results on the ORR activity of the SO2_GQD system is in agreement with the earlier reported results [34] and explaind in details within the supplementary material.

We found that the presence of OH in the S−OH_GQD system leads to a significant amount of charge tranfer both from C0 and C1 atom to adsorb OOH on C1 with a binding enetgy -1.42 eV which is in the desired energy window for efficient ORR. The reduction in electronic density of states near the Fermi level of C0, C1 and C2 after the OOH adsorption is clearly evident from Fig. S3 (b). Here for this S−OH_GQD system the first CPET step i.e. the adsorption of OOH is found to be the potential determining step, as it shows a slight endothermic nature by ∼0.24 V at zero cell potential (shown in Fig. 4 (b)). The onset potential at which all the intermediate reaction steps become exothermic in nature is found to be -0.24 eV, thereby leading to an overpotential value 0.64 eV. This overpotential vaue indicates the suitability of this S−OH_GQD system for ORR considering C1 as the active site.

So, it is indeed exciting to explore that the presence of oxygen or OH can significantly enhance the ability of S dopant to increase the efficiency of ORR on the nearest neighbour carbon atom (C1) of GQD without increasing the doping concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of S-linkers for ORR on entangled GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: After exploring the effect of S-dopant to enhance the catalytic efficiency of a monolayer GQD by varying the position and configuration of the dopant S, we tried to explore the ORR activity of a rather complex system, where two GQD’s are entagled with each other. Furthermore, these two entangled GQDs are connected to each other via two S atoms i.e. S-linkers. Very few reports are available in the literature [33,49] on the role of S-linkers towards the catalytic activity of carbon nanostructures. In the present work we have considered two GQDs each of which consists of total 38 carbon atoms. The edge carbon atoms are passivated with hydrogen in both armchair and zigzag direction. The dimension of each GQD along the armchair direction is ∼9 Å and along the zigzag direction is ∼13 Å. This entire system i.e. the two entangled GQDs connected via S linkers is designated as the 2S_GQD_Ent system. The top view and side view of the full geometry optimized structure of this 2S_GQD_Ent system is shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b) respectively. The S-linkers are attached to the edge of one GQD (GQD_1) and to the centre of the other GQD (GQD_2). Moreover, the S-linkers are substituionally connected (i.e. by replacing two carbon atoms) to the GQD_2. Whereas, with GQD_1 the S-linkers are normally attached not by substituting any carbon atoms from the edge. So, in the resultant 2S_GQD_Ent system the GQD_1 consists of 38 carbon atoms. Whereas, the GQD_2 contains only 36 carbon atoms.

Earlier, in section 3.3 we have found that the substitutionally doped S atom at the centre of a single GQD creats over activity towards the adsorption of OOH and O on the C0 and C1 atoms, which in turn, increases the activation barrier for the OH adsorption enormously, thereby leading to an extremely high overpotential value which is not at all suitable for ORR. Being motivated by this finding, here we have considered the active sites for ORR only on GQD_1 where S-linkers are connected at the edge, not at the centre. In the Fig. 5 (a), C0 indicates the S-linkers. C1, C2, C3 and C4 represents the first, second, third and fourth nearest neighbour carbon atoms respectively of the S-linkers. The bond length between the S linkers and the C1 atoms and i.e., the C0-C1 bond length is found to be ∼1.75 Å after the full geometry optimization.

First, we have tried to explore the ORR activity of C1. We found that the OOH adsorption takes place through the dissociative mechanism (Fig. S4 of the supplementary material). After adsorption the OO bond is completely broken and only one O atom is adsorbed on C1 by forming a strong C1-O bond with bond length 1.24 Å. The separated OH part of the OOH molecule moves towards the C4 atom by making a OO distance even larger than 3 Å. However, unlike the SO2_GQD system this OH is not adsorbed even on C4 also, as the C4-O bond length is found to be ∼3.5 Å. Moreover, due to the strong C1-O bonding, the C1 atom gets detached from C0 i.e., from the S-linker after adsorbing the O atom of the OOH molecule, as shown in Fig. S4 of the supplementary material. So, it is clear that the 2S_GQD_Ent system does not remain stable after the first CPET step, thereby indicating the unsuitability of C1 as the active site for ORR.

Hence, we proceed further by considering C2 as the active site for ORR. Here we found that the OOH adsorption on C2 takes place via associative mechanism with a C2-O bond length ∼1.51 Å. The geometry optimized structure of the 2S_GQD_Ent system after the OOH adsorption on C2 is shown in Fig. S5 of the supplementary material. After adsorption on C2, the OO bond length of OOH is found to be slightly elongated (1.47 Å) as compared to that of a O2 molecule. These C2-O and OO bond length values indicate the moderate binding strength of OOH on C2. In order to understand the ORR activity, we have estimated the change in free energy between all the intermediate reaction steps of ORR on C2 with respect to the SHE. Fig. 5 (c) represents the free energy profile of the 2S_GQD_Ent system along the intermediate steps of ORR in alkaline medium by considering C2 as the active site. We found that the first CPET step (i.e., the OOH adsorption on C2) with zero cell potential shows an endothermic nature by 0.26 eV. However, the second, third and the fourth CPET steps, i.e., the adsorption of O, OH and the desorption of the last OH − ion shows an exothermic nature with downhill in energy by 0.67 eV, 0.39 eV and 0.8 eV respectively. We have also explored the free energy profile along the intermediate steps of ORR with applied cell potential as -0.83 V and 0.4 V, as shown in Fig. 5 (c). So, for this 2S_GQD_Ent system the first CPET step is found to be the potential determining step with an overpotential value 0.66 eV with respect to the SHE.

Since, the first CPET step is found to be the potential determining step, it is highly crucial to explore the interaction characteristics of OOH on C2 in order to undersand the nature of OOH binding. The binding energy of all the intermediates of ORR on C2 is estimated by using the same relation as mentioned in the section 3.2.2. These estimated binding energy values are represented in Table 3 . Furthermore, to understand the nature of OOH binding, we have carried out Bader charge analysis in a similar way as that of the other systems mentioned in the previous sections. The charge states of C0, C1, C2, C3 and C4 before and after the OOH adsorption on C2 are given in Table 4 . From our results in Table 4, it is clear that a significant amount of electronic charge is transferred from the two C0 atoms (S-linkers) as well as from the C2 atom to the OOH in order to adsorb it on C2 with a binding energy ∼ -1.41 eV (given in Table 3). However, no such significant charge transfer from C1, C3 and C4 is found for the OOH adsorption. The estimated binding energy value of OOH on C2 is within the desired energy window as mentioned in section 3.2.2, required for the optimum catalytic activity for ORR. So, the charge transfer from C0 and C2 plays an important role to adsorb the OOH with a moderate binding energy, thereby leading to a suitable ORR activity of C2 with overpotential value 0.66 eV.

Earlier in section 3.2.1, we found that in the 2SE_GQD system no significant amount of charge is transferred to adsorb OOH on C2. As a consequence, the binding energy of OOH on C2 becomes quite low, thereby resulting in a high overpotential value ∼1.29 eV. However, in this 2S_GQD_Ent system the C2 atom is showing reasonable catalytic activity for ORR. This must be attributed to the presence of the other entangled GQD (GQD_2) in which the S-linkers are connected at the centre. In a recent report, Jeon et al. [33] showed that when two graphene nanoplatelets in the same plane are connected edge-to-edge via one sulfur cluster ring (consists of four sulfur atoms), the resultant system shows very limited catalytic activity for ORR. Interestingly, in our 2S_GQD_Ent system where two GQDs are not in the same plane, rather they are entangled or twisted with each other and connected edge (GQD_1) to centre (GQD_2) via S-linkers (consists of two S atoms), the C2 atom shows an appreciable catalytic activity for ORR. So, the twisted or entangled conformation of GQD_2 plays an important role to enhance the ORR activity of GQD_1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of N-doping on the ORR activity of 2S_GQD_Ent system
ce:section.ce:para: Even though the overpotential value on C2 of the 2S_GQD_Ent system is suitable for ORR, it does not indicate very high efficiency for the electrochemical energy conversion. Hence, to further reduce the activation barrier for OOH adsorption, thereby lowering the overpotential value, we have explored the effect of N doping on the catalytic activity of the 2S_GQD_Ent system. A single N atom (corresponding to ∼1.35 % doping concentration) is substitutionally doped at the meta position of C2 i.e., by replacing the C4 atom of the GQD_1. The reason behind choosing the meta position is to avoid the overactivity for the 2nd and 3rd CPET steps i.e., for the O and OH adsorption respectively. This is attributed to the fact that the substitutional N doping is well known to drastically increase the activity of the nearest neighbour carbon atom at the ortho position. After N doping the resultant system is designated as the N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent system and the doped N atom is designated as CN.

We have explored the free energy profile of the N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent system along the intermediate steps of ORR in alkaline medium by considering C2 as the active site, which is shown in Fig. 6 . Our results on the free energy analysis clearly indicates that in the first CPET step the activation barrier for OOH adsorption is reduced to 0.11 eV after N doping. This is a significant improvement over that of the 2S-GQD_Ent system. In this N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent system the 2nd, 3rd and the 4th CPET steps show an exothermic nature with downhill in energy by 1.3 eV, 0.09 eV and 0.32 eV respectively. Our results on the Bader charge analysis (shown in Table 4) indicates that a significant amount of charge is transferred to OOH from C0, C1, C2 as well as from C3 to adsorb OOH on C2 with a binding energy ∼ -1.56 eV (shown in Table 3). So, due to this combined contribution of C0, C1, C2 and C3 in this N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent system more charge is transferred to OOH as compared to that of the 2S-GQD_Ent system. This charge transfer is further verified by the electronic structure analysis. Fig. 7 represents the sum DOS of C0, C1, C2, C3 and C4/CN before and after the OOH adsorption on C2 for (a) 2S-GQD_Ent and (b) N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent systems. From Fig. 7 (a) and (b) it is clearly evident that the reduction in electronic density of states near the Fermi level after OOH adsorption is reasonably higher in N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent system as compared to that of the 2S-GQD_Ent system. As a consequence, the binding energy of OOH on C2 is also higher for N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent system, thereby reducing the activation barrier to adsorb OOH in the first CPET step, as compared to that of the 2S-GQD_Ent system. Moreover, the estimated binding energy of OOH is exactly in the middle of the desired energy window and indicates the optimum binding of OOH for efficient ORR. Here, the first CPET step in the free energy profile is the potential determining step with an overpotential value 0.51 eV with respect to SHE. This estimated overpotential value indicates very high catalytic efficiency for ORR, as this value is comparable to that of the Pt-based metal catalysts. Moreover, it can be inferred that the combined effect of entangled GQD, S-linkers and a single N dopant drastically enhances the catalytic activity of GQD which is showing even better efficiency as compared to that of the earlier reported results where multiple N atoms [50] or N and S atoms together were doped on a single graphene system.

Hence, our results on the ORR activity of a single as well as two entangle GQDs indicate towards the possibility of utilizing the 2SE_GQD, SO2_GQD and N-doped-2S-GQD_Ent systems as the potential metal-free electrocatalysts for highly efficient ORR in the practical application.

The role of S-dopant as well as S-linker in enhancing the catalytic activity of GQD for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is explored here successfully via density functional theory-based approach. Interestingly, when doping concentration of S becomes 2.94 % i.e., double S dopants is considered at the edge, the ORR efficiency is found to be enhanced drastically with an overpotential value as low as 0.52 eV. Almost similar improvement in ORR efficiency is found when S is doped at the edge in presence of O2 or OH. This enhancement in ORR efficiency is attributed to the optimum charge transfer from the dopant site to the neighbouring active sites to adsorb the intermediates with moderate binding energy values required for successfully facilitating the ORR. Furthermore, the S-linkers are exploited to connect via GQD_1 edge to the GQD_2 centre when the two GQDs entangled or twisted with each other. This novel complex system is found to exhibit ORR activity with an enhanced efficiency and very low overpotential value (0.51 eV) in presence of a single N dopant. The twisted or entangled conformation of GQD_2 plays a significant role in enhancing the ORR activity of GQD_1 by tuning the electronic structure near to the Fermi level. Overall, we conclude that our results indicate towards the possibility of developing metal-free and cost-effective potential electrocatalyst for ORR using simply the S-dopant and the S-linkers on the GQD surface, which shows a high efficiency and low overpotential value and is comparable to the conventionally used Pt-based metal catalysts.

Paramita Banerjee: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. G.P. Das: Funding acquisition, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization. Ranjit Thapa: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: PB and GPD thankfully acknowledges IACS for the financial and computational support. R.T. thanks Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences (BRNS), India, (Grant Nos. 37(2)/20/14/2018-BRNS/37144) and Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), India, (Grant Nos. EMR/2016/004689) for the financial support. Authors thank High Performance Computing Centre, SRM Institute of Science and Technology for providing the computational facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cattod.2021.03.001.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article: Some complementary figures and tables e.g. geometry optimized structure of the Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) considered in the present study, free energy profile along the intermediate steps of ORR in alkaline medium on the SC_GQD and SE_GQD systems, sum DOS of C0, C1 and C2 before and after OOH adsorption on C1 for SO2_GQD and S−OH_GQD systems, the top view and side view of the geometry optimized structures of 2S_GQD_Ent system after OOH adsorption on C1 and on C2, the Bader charge state of C0, C1 and C2 atoms on pristine GQD system as well as on the SC_GQD, SE_GQD, SO2_GQD and S−OH_GQD systems before and after the OOH adsorption and also the binding energy of the intermediates of ORR on the active sites of the SC_GQD, SE_GQD, SO2_GQD and S−OH_GQD systems are shown clearly.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-free carbon nanomaterials become more active than metal catalysts and last longer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Yu, E. Nagelli, F. Du, L. Dai, Metal-free carbon nanomaterials become more active than metal catalysts and last longer, J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 1 (2010) 2165-2173.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-free catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Dai, Y. Xue, L. Qu, H.J. Choi, J.B. Baek, Metal-free catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction, Chem. Rev. 115 (2015) 4823-4892.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Factors governing oxygen reduction in solid oxide fuel cell cathodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.B. Adler, Factors governing oxygen reduction in solid oxide fuel cell cathodes, Chem. Rev. 104 (2004) 4791-4843.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shao, Q. Chang, J.P. Dodelet, R. Chenitz, Recent advances in electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction, Chem. Rev. 116 (2016) 3594-3657.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-throughput screening of binary catalysts for oxygen electroreduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.H. Liu, M.K Jeon, S.I. Woo, High-throughput screening of binary catalysts for oxygen electroreduction, App. Surf. Sci. 252 (2006) 2580-2587.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exploring adsorption and desorption characteristics of molecular hydrogen on neutral and charged Mg nanoclusters: a first principles study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Banerjee, K.R.S. Chandrakumar, G.P. Das, Exploring adsorption and desorption characteristics of molecular hydrogen on neutral and charged Mg nanoclusters: a first principles study, Chem. Phys. 469 (2016) 123-131.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: First-principles identification of the origin for higher activity of surface doped carbon nanohorn: impact on hydrogen storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Banerjee, R. Thapa, A. Rajkamal, K.R.S. Chandrakumar, G.P. Das, First-principles identification of the origin for higher activity of surface doped carbon nanohorn: impact on hydrogen storage, Int. J. Hyd. Energy 44 (2019) 23196-23209.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dehydrogenation characteristics of MgnH2n (n = 1-32) nanoclusters: a first-principles DFT study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Banerjee, K.R.S. Chandrakumar, G.P. Das, Dehydrogenation characteristics of MgnH2n (n = 1-32) nanoclusters: a first-principles DFT study. AIP Conf. Proc. 1665 (2015) 050065.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 3d-transition metal induced enhancement of molecular hydrogen adsorption on Mg (0001) surface: an ab-initio study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Banerjee, G.P. Das, 3d-transition metal induced enhancement of molecular hydrogen adsorption on Mg (0001) surface: an ab-initio study. AIP Conf. Proc. 1731 (2016) 080028.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heteroatom-doped graphitic carbon catalysts for efficient electrocatalysis of oxygen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, L. Da, Heteroatom-doped graphitic carbon catalysts for efficient electrocatalysis of oxygen reduction reaction, ACS Catal. 5 (2015) 7244-7253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ammonia-treated ordered mesoporous carbons as catalytic materials for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, J.S. Lee, Q. Zhu, J. Liu, Y. Wang, S. Dai, Ammonia-treated ordered mesoporous carbons as catalytic materials for oxygen reduction reaction, Chem. Mater. 22 (2010) 2178-2180.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exploring the catalytic activity of pristine T6[100] surface for oxygen reduction reaction: a first-principles study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Banerjee, S. Chakrabarty, R. Thapa, G.P. Das, Exploring the catalytic activity of pristine T6[100] surface for oxygen reduction reaction: a first-principles study, Appl. Surf. Sci. 418 (2017) 56-63.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorine-doped carbon blacks: highly efficient metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Sun, Y. Zhang, P. Song, J. Pan, L. Zhuang, W. Xu, W. Xing, Fluorine-doped carbon blacks: highly efficient metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction, ACS Catal. 3 (2013) 1726-1729.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Porous core-shell Fe3C embedded N-doped carbon nanofibers as an effective electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Ren, X. Lu, Y. Li, Y. Zhu, L. Dai, L. Jiang, Porous core-shell Fe3C embedded N-doped carbon nanofibers as an effective electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8 (2016) 4118-4125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticle-carbon nanofiber composite as an efficient metal-free cathode catalyst for oxygen reduction reaction

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped graphene as efficient metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction in fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Qu, Y. Liu, J.B. Baek, L. Dai, Nitrogen-doped graphene as efficient metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction in fuel cells, ACS Nano 4 (2010) 1321-1326.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of microstructure of nitrogen-doped graphene on oxygen reduction activity in fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, J. Niu, L. Dai, Z. Xia, Effect of microstructure of nitrogen-doped graphene on oxygen reduction activity in fuel cells, Langmuir 28 (2012) 7542-7550.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxygen reduction reactions on pure and nitrogen-doped graphene: a first-principles modelling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. W. Boukhvalov, Y.W. Son, Oxygen reduction reactions on pure and nitrogen-doped graphene: a first-principles modelling, Nanoscale 4 (2012) 417-420.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanisms of oxygen reduction reaction on nitrogen-doped graphene for fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, Z. Xia, Mechanisms of oxygen reduction reaction on nitrogen-doped graphene for fuel cells, J. Phys. Chem. C 115 (2011) 11170–11176.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DFT study of stabilization effects on N-doped graphene for ORR catalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Reda, H.A. Hansen, T. Vegge, DFT study of stabilization effects on N-doped graphene for ORR catalysis, Catal. Today 312 (2018) 118-125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of subsurface vacancies on oxygen reduction reaction activity of Pt-based alloys

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Callejas-Tovar, P.B. Balbuena, Effect of subsurface vacancies on oxygen reduction reaction activity of Pt-based alloys, J. Phys. Chem. C 116 (2012) 14414-14422.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrocatalysis on platinum nanoparticles: particle size effect on oxygen reduction reaction activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shao, A. Peles, K. Shoemaker, Electrocatalysis on platinum nanoparticles: particle size effect on oxygen reduction reaction activity, Nano Lett. 11 (2011) 3714-3719.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: GLAD Pt−Ni alloy nanorods for oxygen reduction reaction
